text:
*[Bug 18999] New: <http://rep.int|rep.int> and rep_len on factor do not drop names attribute*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18999>

            Bug ID: 18999
           Summary: <http://rep.int|rep.int> and rep_len on factor do not drop names
                    attribute
           Product: R
           Version: R 4.5.x
          Hardware: All
               URL: <https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/r-devel/2026-January/08>
                    4347.html
                OS: All
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: minor
          Priority: P5
         Component: Misc
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:suharto_anggono@yahoo.com|suharto_anggono@yahoo.com>

In <https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/r-devel/2026-January/084347.html> by Rui
Barradas:

In the documentation for ?rep, sections Value and Note say that


 > Value
 > An object of the same type as x.

 > <http://rep.int|rep.int> and rep_len return no attributes (except the class if 
returning a factor).

 > The default method of rep gives the result names (which will almost 
always contain duplicates) if x had names, but retains no other 
attributes.


 > Note
 > Function <http://rep.int|rep.int> is a simple case which was provided as a separate 
function partly for S compatibility and partly for speed (especially 
when names can be dropped). The performance of rep has been improved 
since, but <http://rep.int|rep.int> is still at least twice as fast when x has names.


And this works as documented.


y <- 1:4
names(y) <- letters[1:4]
class(y) <- "new_class"
attr(y, "this") <- "that"

y
#> a b c d
#> 1 2 3 4
#> attr(,"class")
#> [1] "new_class"
#> attr(,"this")
#> [1] "that"

rep(y, 2)      # keeps names only
#> a b c d a b c d
#> 1 2 3 4 1 2 3 4

<http://rep.int|rep.int>(y, 2)  # no names and no other attributes
#> [1] 1 2 3 4 1 2 3 4

rep_len(y, 2)  # no names and no other attributes
#> [1] 1 2



But in the documentation's last example, the factor example, the names 
attribute is not dropped.



## named factor
x <- factor(LETTERS[1:4]); names(x) <- letters[1:4]
x
#> a b c d
#> A B C D
#> Levels: A B C D

rep(x, 2)
#> a b c d a b c d
#> A B C D A B C D
#> Levels: A B C D

rep(x, each = 2)
#> a a b b c c d d
#> A A B B C C D D
#> Levels: A B C D

<http://rep.int|rep.int>(x, 2)  # no names  <--- Wrong comment
#> a b c d a b c d
#> A B C D A B C D
#> Levels: A B C D

rep_len(x, 10)
#> a b c d a b c d a b
#> A B C D A B C D A B
#> Levels: A B C D



Further test with attributes()



<http://rep.int|rep.int>(x, 2) |> attributes()
#> $names
#> [1] "a" "b" "c" "d" "a" "b" "c" "d"
#>
#> $class
#> [1] "factor"
#>
#> $levels
#> [1] "A" "B" "C" "D"

rep_len(x, 10) |> attributes()
#> $names
#>  [1] "a" "b" "c" "d" "a" "b" "c" "d" "a" "b"
#>
#> $class
#> [1] "factor"
#>
#> $levels
#> [1] "A" "B" "C" "D"



If I'm reading the docs right, this is wrong, isn't it?


=====================
In <https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/r-devel/2026-January/084356.html> by Sebastian
Meyer:

This is a consequence of the following change in R 4.0.0 (r77905):

     • When internal dispatch for ‘<http://rep.int|rep.int>()’ and ‘rep_len()’
       fails, there is an attempt to dispatch on the equivalent
       call to ‘rep()’.

I believe it wasn't intended to change the handling of factors and I 
think it would be desirable to restore the original (and still 
documented) behavior of dropping names for <http://rep.int|rep.int>(<factor>) and 
rep_len(<factor>), so skip the (early) 'rep' fallback if `inherits(s, 
"factor")`.

.... 
At a first glance the patch looks simple (and should include a 
regression test).

by Rui
Barradas:

In the documentation for ?rep, sections Value and Note say that



 > Value
 > An object of the same type as x.

 >

and rep_len return no attributes (except the class if 
returning a factor).

 > The default method of rep gives the result names (which will almost 
always contain duplicates) if x had names, but retains no other 
attributes.



 > Note
 > Function

is a simple case which was provided as a separate 
function partly for S compatibility and partly for speed (especially 
when names can be dropped). The performance of rep has been improved 
since, but

is still at least twice as fast when x has names.



And this works as documented.

y
#> a b c d
#> 1 2 3 4
#> attr(,"class")
#> [1] "new_class"
#> attr(,"this")
#> [1] "that"

rep(y, 2)      # keeps names only
#> a b c d a b c d
#> 1 2 3 4 1 2 3 4

(y, 2)  # no names and no other attributes
#> [1] 1 2 3 4 1 2 3 4

rep_len(y, 2)  # no names and no other attributes
#> [1] 1 2





But in the documentation's last example, the factor example, the names 
attribute is not dropped.





## named factor
x <- factor(LETTERS[1:4]); names(x) <- letters[1:4]
x
#> a b c d
#> A B C D
#> Levels: A B C D

rep(x, 2)
#> a b c d a b c d
#> A B C D A B C D
#> Levels: A B C D

rep(x, each = 2)
#> a a b b c c d d
#> A A B B C C D D
#> Levels: A B C D

(x, 2)  # no names  <--- Wrong comment
#> a b c d a b c d
#> A B C D A B C D
#> Levels: A B C D

rep_len(x, 10)
#> a b c d a b c d a b
#> A B C D A B C D A B
#> Levels: A B C D





Further test with attributes()

(x, 2) |> attributes()
#> $names
#> [1] "a" "b" "c" "d" "a" "b" "c" "d"
#>
#> $class
#> [1] "factor"
#>
#> $levels
#> [1] "A" "B" "C" "D"

rep_len(x, 10) |> attributes()
#> $names
#>  [1] "a" "b" "c" "d" "a" "b" "c" "d" "a" "b"
#>
#> $class
#> [1] "factor"
#>
#> $levels
#> [1] "A" "B" "C" "D"





If I'm reading the docs right, this is wrong, isn't it?

text:
*[Bug 19000] New: max/min/range for numeric_version doesn't respect na.rm = TRUE*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19000>

            Bug ID: 19000
           Summary: max/min/range for numeric_version doesn't respect
                    na.rm = TRUE
           Product: R
           Version: R 4.5.x
          Hardware: x86_64/x64/amd64 (64-bit)
                OS: Linux
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: normal
          Priority: P5
         Component: Low-level
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:marberts@protonmail.com|marberts@protonmail.com>

Dear R Core,

The following should not return NA.

```
x <- as.numeric_version(c("1.1", NA))
max(x, na.rm = TRUE)
> [1] <NA>
```

The same happens with `min()` and `range()`. The `Summary()` group generic for
the `numeric_version` class doesn't remove NA's from `max()/min()` in the
branch when `na.rm = TRUE`, resulting in the min/max always being
`x[integer(0)[1]]`.
